title: Housing Crisis in Focus: LIHTC Best Practices to Discourage Qualified Contracts and Keep Housing Affordable for Longer
summary: This Treasury Department blog post discusses best practices for state allocating agencies to discourage owners of Low-Income Housing Tax Credit (LIHTC) properties from using the Qualified Contract provision to exit affordability covenants early. The post highlights that the Qualified Contract provision, designed as an investor exit option, has resulted in the loss of 6,000-10,000 affordable housing units annually as the statutory pricing formula often exceeds market value. Treasury encourages states to adopt waiver requirements or point-based incentive systems to preserve affordability beyond the initial 15-year compliance period.
key_points: LIHTC is the federal government's largest tool for affordable housing supply, with approximately 25% of new apartments between 2000-2019 supported by LIHTCs.
The Qualified Contract provision allows owners after year 14 to request that state agencies find buyers at a statutorily defined price; if no buyer is found within one year, affordability ends.
The statutory qualified contract pricing formula often far exceeds market value of restricted properties, enabling early exit from affordability commitments.
Experts estimate 6,000-10,000 low-income units are lost annually through the qualified contract process; approximately 115,000 units lost total over time.
Dozens of state agencies now require new LIHTC applicants to waive their right to request a qualified contract as a condition of receiving credit allocations.
Some states use point-based Qualified Allocation Plan systems to incentivize longer waiver periods (5, 10, or 15 years) for qualified contract rights.
why_it_matters: This source describes Treasury's policy guidance for state agencies regarding LIHTC program administration. It does not directly discuss market-moving macro catalysts or market structure elements. It is a policy best practices document for housing program administration, with no direct implications for financial market participants or trading strategies.
important_numbers: 25% of new apartments (2000-2019) supported by LIHTCs
6,000-10,000 affordable units lost annually via qualified contracts
115,000 total units lost historically through qualified contracts
14th year marks earliest qualified contract eligibility
